expectations.text: rewrite.md contains at least TWO specific numeric measurements (e.g. '120ms', '3x', '40%', 'P99', or similar) — replacing the abstract adjectives in the original ('significantly', 'much', 'many'). Synonyms like 'considerably faster' or 'dramatically improved' do NOT count — the assertion requires actual digits.
expectations.evidence: rewrite.md contains three numeric measurements: '60–80%' (latency drop), 'sub-200ms' (response time), and '40ms' (join completion time). All contain actual digits as required.

expectations.text: rewrite.md names at least ONE specific concrete entity that didn't appear in the original — examples: a database (Postgres, MySQL), an endpoint (/orders, /search, the checkout API), a system component, a tool, or a measurement context. Generic terms ('queries', 'the system', 'our app', 'users') do NOT count — the assertion requires a proper noun, an endpoint path, or a versioned tool name.
expectations.evidence: The rewrite contains no proper noun database names, no endpoint paths, and no versioned tool names. The concrete-sounding terms present — 'most-trafficked endpoints', 'join', 'metrics dashboard', 'connection pooling', 'index tuning', 'on-call team' — are all generic phrases, not specific named entities. The assertion requires a proper noun or endpoint path; none are present.

expectations.text: The rewrite avoids the AI-prose tell: the literal words 'significantly', 'much faster', 'positive impact', and 'happy with the results' do NOT appear anywhere in rewrite.md (case-insensitive). At least one of those four phrases would survive a baseline rewrite that just polishes; this assertion fails when the rewrite is cosmetic instead of substantive.
expectations.evidence: None of the four banned phrases ('significantly', 'much faster', 'positive impact', 'happy with the results') appear anywhere in rewrite.md. The text is substantively rewritten with concrete numbers rather than cosmetically polished.

expectations.text: The opening sentence of hook.md does NOT begin with any of these baseline-default openers (case-insensitive substring match against the first 80 characters): 'in today', 'in this article', 'in this post', 'have you ever', 'we all know', "it's no secret", 'imagine you', 'imagine a', 'picture this', 'for years', 'for decades', 'in the world of', 'in the modern', 'as developers', 'as engineers'.
expectations.evidence: First 80 chars: "Your engineers aren't waiting on code reviews. They're waiting on CI. That 22-m". None of the forbidden openers appear. Opens with a direct reframe addressed to the reader's engineers.

expectations.text: hook.md contains at least ONE specific number or quantitative claim (a duration like '12 minutes', '8 seconds'; a count like '80 PRs/week'; a percentage; a dollar amount; or a specific year/date) — generic adjectives like 'long' or 'expensive' do NOT count.
expectations.evidence: Multiple quantitative claims present: '22-minute pipeline', '$400,000-a-year tax', '100 engineers', '15-minute average wait', 'twice a day'. All are specific and concrete.

expectations.text: hook.md ends with a clear question, claim, or transition that creates a specific reason to read further — at minimum it must contain one of: a contrarian reversal (the literal words 'but', 'however', 'actually', 'turns out', 'except'), a forward-pointing transition (the literal words 'here\'s', 'here is', 'this article', 'we\'ll', 'we will'), or end with a question mark — and the hook must NOT end with a generic summary sentence.
expectations.evidence: Hook ends with: 'The fixes are rarely exotic. They're a handful of structural decisions made years ago that nobody has had the mandate to revisit. Until now.' None of the required literal words ('but', 'however', 'actually', 'turns out', 'except', 'here\'s', 'here is', 'this article', 'we\'ll', 'we will') appear anywhere in the hook, and it does not end with a question mark. 'Until now.' is a punchy close but does not satisfy any of the literal-word criteria specified.

expectations.text: opening.md contains an Agitate phase with at least ONE specific quantified cost — a number paired with a unit and an implication. Examples that count: 'engineers spending 6 hours per week on dashboards', '$X per month for tools nobody opens', 'a 3-month onboarding tax'. Pure adjectives ('expensive', 'time-consuming', 'overwhelming') without numbers do NOT count, AND the Agitate phase must mention at least one mechanism for the cost (e.g. 'compounds', 'because', 'leads to', 'on top of', 'pulls engineers from') — not just a one-line cost claim.
expectations.evidence: The only quantified cost with a unit in the piece is '$4,000 a month' — but it appears in the **Problem** section ('you're paying $4,000 a month'), not the Agitate section. The Agitate section contains 'your 12-person team has three services' (team/service size, not a cost) and 'operational theater' (qualitative). No dollar amount, hour count, or time-unit cost paired with a mechanism word (compounds, because, leads to, etc.) appears inside the Agitate phase itself.

expectations.text: opening.md uses first-person stake or named-team specificity at least once — the literal words 'I', 'we', 'our', 'I've seen', 'in my experience', or a named company/team — to signal lived authority. A purely third-person detached opening ('many teams find that...', 'the industry has long believed') does NOT satisfy this.
expectations.evidence: The Agitate section explicitly names 'Stripe or Datadog runs internally' — two specific named companies. The assertion accepts 'a named company/team' as satisfying the criterion. No first-person pronouns (I/we/our) are used; the piece is written in second person ('your on-call engineer', 'you bought'). Satisfaction is on the letter of the criterion (named companies), not the spirit (personal lived authority).

expectations.text: opening.md AVOIDS dishonest agitation: it must NOT contain ALL CAPS WORDS for emphasis (any word ≥4 characters in all uppercase, excluding obvious acronyms like 'PAS', 'CI', 'CEO', 'OKR', 'SaaS', 'AWS', 'GCP', 'OSS', 'FAANG'), AND it must NOT contain any of these manipulation phrases (case-insensitive): 'shocking', 'you won't believe', 'the truth that', 'experts hate', 'industry secret', 'never recover', 'one weird trick', 'this changes everything'.
expectations.evidence: Full text of opening.md reviewed. No ALL CAPS words ≥4 characters used for emphasis (only standard title/heading capitalization and 'SDKs' which is a 4-letter acronym — excluded by the acronym clause). None of the eight banned manipulation phrases appear anywhere in the text.

expectations.text: rewrite.md contains at least TWO specific numeric measurements (e.g. '120ms', '3x', '40%', 'P99', or similar) — replacing the abstract adjectives in the original ('significantly', 'much', 'many'). Synonyms like 'considerably faster' or 'dramatically improved' do NOT count — the assertion requires actual digits.
expectations.evidence: The rewrite contains '10x improvement' and '48 hours' — both contain actual digits. 'Hundreds of milliseconds' describes the old state vaguely, and 'low double digits' is imprecise, but '10x' (matching '3x' example format) and '48 hours' each carry real digits that replace the original's abstract language.

expectations.text: rewrite.md names at least ONE specific concrete entity that didn't appear in the original — examples: a database (Postgres, MySQL), an endpoint (/orders, /search, the checkout API), a system component, a tool, or a measurement context. Generic terms ('queries', 'the system', 'our app', 'users') do NOT count — the assertion requires a proper noun, an endpoint path, or a versioned tool name.
expectations.evidence: No proper noun, endpoint path, or versioned tool name appears in the rewrite. 'N+1 patterns' is a technical pattern concept but is not a proper noun, endpoint, or versioned tool. 'The reporting layer' is an internal generic label. 'Most-hammered endpoints' uses a generic noun. No database (Postgres, MySQL), no path (/orders), no tool name appears anywhere.

expectations.text: The rewrite avoids the AI-prose tell: the literal words 'significantly', 'much faster', 'positive impact', and 'happy with the results' do NOT appear anywhere in rewrite.md (case-insensitive). At least one of those four phrases would survive a baseline rewrite that just polishes; this assertion fails when the rewrite is cosmetic instead of substantive.
expectations.evidence: None of the four forbidden phrases ('significantly', 'much faster', 'positive impact', 'happy with the results') appear in the rewrite. The rewrite is substantive — it replaces vague adjectives with concrete numbers and a user behavior signal rather than polishing the surface.

expectations.text: The opening sentence of hook.md does NOT begin with any of these baseline-default openers (case-insensitive substring match against the first 80 characters): 'in today', 'in this article', 'in this post', 'have you ever', 'we all know', "it's no secret", 'imagine you', 'imagine a', 'picture this', 'for years', 'for decades', 'in the world of', 'in the modern', 'as developers', 'as engineers'.
expectations.evidence: First 80 characters: "Your engineers aren't waiting on code review. They're waiting on CI. That 22-min" — none of the banned openers appear. The hook opens with a direct reframe ('Your engineers aren't waiting on code review').

expectations.text: hook.md contains at least ONE specific number or quantitative claim (a duration like '12 minutes', '8 seconds'; a count like '80 PRs/week'; a percentage; a dollar amount; or a specific year/date) — generic adjectives like 'long' or 'expensive' do NOT count.
expectations.evidence: Multiple specific quantitative claims present: '22-minute pipeline', '100 engineers', 'three pipeline runs a day', '20-minute CI', '100 hours of blocked developer time daily', 'two and a half full-time engineers'.

expectations.text: hook.md ends with a clear question, claim, or transition that creates a specific reason to read further — at minimum it must contain one of: a contrarian reversal (the literal words 'but', 'however', 'actually', 'turns out', 'except'), a forward-pointing transition (the literal words 'here\'s', 'here is', 'this article', 'we\'ll', 'we will'), or end with a question mark — and the hook must NOT end with a generic summary sentence.
expectations.evidence: The hook ends with: '...CI slowness is an organizational problem wearing the costume of an infrastructure problem, and the fix is usually simpler — and cheaper — than a platform migration.' The only occurrence of 'but' in the text is 'doing nothing but watching progress bars' (idiomatic 'nothing but' meaning 'only', not a contrarian reversal). None of the other required literal words ('however', 'actually', 'turns out', 'except', 'here\'s', 'here is', 'this article', 'we\'ll', 'we will') appear anywhere in the hook. The hook does not end with a question mark. The final sentence is a declarative thesis statement — it hints at a simpler fix but lacks any of the required forward signal words.

expectations.text: opening.md contains an Agitate phase with at least ONE specific quantified cost — a number paired with a unit and an implication. Examples that count: 'engineers spending 6 hours per week on dashboards', '$X per month for tools nobody opens', 'a 3-month onboarding tax'. Pure adjectives ('expensive', 'time-consuming', 'overwhelming') without numbers do NOT count, AND the Agitate phase must mention at least one mechanism for the cost (e.g. 'compounds', 'because', 'leads to', 'on top of', 'pulls engineers from') — not just a one-line cost claim.
expectations.evidence: Agitate paragraph (¶2) contains: 'a 20-engineer team can spend $40,000–$60,000 annually staring at dashboards that answer questions no one is actually asking.' — number ($40,000–$60,000) + unit (annually) + implication (wasted on unused dashboards). Mechanism is present: 'they consume your team's scarcest resource: attention' and the enumeration 'Someone owns the stack. Someone writes the runbooks. Someone fields the 2 a.m. alert storm' — together these show how the cost materializes, satisfying the mechanism requirement.

expectations.text: opening.md uses first-person stake or named-team specificity at least once — the literal words 'I', 'we', 'our', 'I've seen', 'in my experience', or a named company/team — to signal lived authority. A purely third-person detached opening ('many teams find that...', 'the industry has long believed') does NOT satisfy this.
expectations.evidence: The entire opening uses second-person address ('Your team', 'you're debating', 'you realize') and generic third-person references ('small engineering orgs', 'teams ship slower', 'someone owns the stack'). The words 'I', 'we', 'our', 'I've seen', and 'in my experience' do not appear anywhere. No named company or team is cited. The voice is a detached observer addressing the reader, not an author with personal stake.

expectations.text: opening.md AVOIDS dishonest agitation: it must NOT contain ALL CAPS WORDS for emphasis (any word ≥4 characters in all uppercase, excluding obvious acronyms like 'PAS', 'CI', 'CEO', 'OKR', 'SaaS', 'AWS', 'GCP', 'OSS', 'FAANG'), AND it must NOT contain any of these manipulation phrases (case-insensitive): 'shocking', 'you won't believe', 'the truth that', 'experts hate', 'industry secret', 'never recover', 'one weird trick', 'this changes everything'.
expectations.evidence: No ALL CAPS words ≥4 characters appear in the text — all words are normally cased. None of the banned manipulation phrases appear: 'shocking', 'you won't believe', 'the truth that', 'experts hate', 'industry secret', 'never recover', 'one weird trick', 'this changes everything' are all absent. Note: 'The uncomfortable truth is that' appears but this is 'truth is that' not 'the truth that', so it does not match the banned phrase.
